elements.sent-2411-001: Few-shot segmentation~(FSS) aims to segment the target object in a query image using only a small set of support images and masks.
elements.sent-2411-002: Therefore, having strong prior information for the target object using the support set is essential for guiding the initial training of FSS, which leads to the success of few-shot segmentation in challenging cases, such as when the target object shows considerable variation in appearance, texture, or scale across the support and query images.
elements.sent-2411-003: Previous methods have tried to obtain prior information by creating correlation maps from pixel-level correlation on final-layer or same-layer features.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, we found these approaches can offer limited and partial information when advanced models like Vision Transformers are used as the backbone.
elements.sent-2411-005: Vision Transformer encoders have a multi-layer structure with identical shapes in their intermediate layers.
elements.sent-2411-006: Leveraging the feature comparison from all layers in the encoder can enhance the performance of few-shot segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-007: We introduce FCC~(Fully Connected Correlation) to integrate pixel-level correlations between support and query features, capturing associations that reveal target-specific patterns and correspondences in both same-layers and cross-layers.
elements.sent-2411-008: FCC captures previously inaccessible target information, effectively addressing the limitations of support mask.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our approach consistently demonstrates state-of-the-art performance on PASCAL, COCO, and domain shift tests.
elements.sent-2411-010: We conducted an ablation study and cross-layer correlation analysis to validate FCC's core methodology.
elements.sent-2411-011: These findings reveal the effectiveness of FCC in enhancing prior information and overall model performance.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[height=6.4cm]{sec/figures/teaser2.png} \caption{The proposed method, FCC, demonstrates precise segmentation in challenging scenarios on COCO-20$^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft} compared to baseline~(DSCM~\cite{dscm}): (a) \textcolor{orange}{Scale Difference}: where the target object appears at different scales in the query and support images; (b) \textcolor{orange}{Scale Difference} + \textcolor{purple}{Occlusion}: where the target object is partially occluded by other objects, with only part of it visible in the support set; (c) \textcolor{orange}{Scale Difference} + \textcolor{purple}{Occlusion} + \textcolor{teal}{Shape Difference}: where objects of the same class differ in color and shape, as seen in various dog breeds; (d) \textcolor{orange}{Scale Difference} + \textcolor{purple}{Occlusion} + \textcolor{teal}{Shape Difference} + \textcolor{red}{Limited Information}: the most challenging case, where minimal information is provided, and all previous challenges are present simultaneously. The green circle highlights the area of significant improvement with FCC.} \label{fig:4cases}
elements.sent-2411-012: The advancement of deep learning has significantly improved object segmentation~\cite{FCN, Deeplab, pyramidscene , enabling its application across diverse fields such as healthcare, biology, semiconductor manufacturing, and autonomous driving.}
elements.sent-2411-013: The improvements in object segmentation have largely been achieved through supervised learning.
elements.sent-2411-014: Despite these advances, fully supervised segmentation methods face limitations in many of these fields due to data scarcity and the challenges of acquiring high-quality, annotated datasets across varied conditions and specialized applications~\cite{song2023comprehensive, alzubaidi2023scarcity .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Few-shot learning has emerged as an active area of research to address this problem, aiming to achieve strong performance with a small amount of data~\cite{snell2017prototypical,wang2020generalizingexamplessurveyfewshot, song2023comprehensive .}
elements.sent-2411-016: By leveraging few-shot learning, the substantial costs associated with data acquisition and labeling can be reduced, significantly expanding the potential for segmentation applications in fields like biology, electronics, and satellite imaging~\cite{lin2023few_micai,fewshot_elec,fewshot_satelite .}
elements.sent-2411-017: In few-shot segmentation, only a few examples with target object masks are provided.
elements.sent-2411-018: Using this limited information, we need to segment the target object within a new image, known as the query image.
elements.sent-2411-019: This task is believed to be introduced by~\citet{OSLSM for the first time.}
elements.sent-2411-020: Since then, numerous approaches have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-021: Most previous FSS works utilize pixel-level correlation between query and support features to generate prior mask or aggregate support information \cite{rethinking, HSNet, ASNet, vrpsam, dscm, hmmasking,msi, VAT .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Most of the previous works generate a prior mask using a correlation map.
elements.sent-2411-023: The correlation map plays a crucial role by providing strong prior information to the network.
elements.sent-2411-024: As a result, the correlation map is a key component in few-shot segmentation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Pixel-level correlation in previous methods is mostly computed comparing features from the same layer in a network~\cite{msi,hmmasking,VAT,HSNet,ASNet,piclip,vrpsam .}
elements.sent-2411-026: However, relying only on same-layer features could miss nuanced details about target information, especially when objects' appearance, scale, or structure in the support and query images differ considerably.
elements.sent-2411-027: This could lead to a performance bottleneck in FSS~(Fig.~\ref{fig:4cases ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Earlier research has overlooked the potential benefits of cross-layer correlations for generating pixel-level correlations~(Fig.~\ref{fig:comparison_conv_fcc ).}
elements.sent-2411-029: Comparing features across different layers has often been considered counterintuitive.
elements.sent-2411-030: In convolutional neural networks~(CNN) such as ResNet~\cite{Resnet , lower layers are understood to capture low-level image features, while higher layers progressively capture more abstract information.}
elements.sent-2411-031: This means each layer represents distinct types of information, making comparing same-layer features reasonable.
elements.sent-2411-032: Therefore, this same-layer pixel-level correlation is considered as defacto in FSS.
elements.sent-2411-033: This concept is adopted to the Vision Transformer~(ViT)~\cite{vit architecture, with the assumption that ViT exhibits a similar hierarchical understanding.}
elements.sent-2411-034: However, \citet{raghu2021vision observed significant differences between ViT~\cite{vit and CNN.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: ViT captures more uniform representations with greater similarity between lower and upper layers than CNN.
elements.sent-2411-036: Therefore, features from ViT are less hierarchical than convolutional neural networks, making comparisons across different ViT layers a reasonable approach for uncovering detailed information at various levels.
elements.sent-2411-037: Also, the architecture of ViT allows to compute cross-layer correlation directly.
elements.sent-2411-038: Features from each layer in ViT maintain the same width and height with the same channels, making comparisons between different layers possible.
elements.sent-2411-039: The architecture of ResNet~\cite{Resnet does not naturally support cross-layer comparisons.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Due to pooling operations and convolution blocks with different channel sizes, features from different layers in ResNet have different channel dimensions and spatial resolutions.
elements.sent-2411-041: In this paper, we present Fully Connected Correlation~(FCC), a novel approach designed to enhance few-shot segmentation by leveraging cross-layer correlations in addition to traditional same-layer comparisons.
elements.sent-2411-042: Unlike prior methods that typically rely on correlation maps generated from features at the same layer, FCC integrates correlations from multiple levels of the Vision Transformer~(ViT) encoder.
elements.sent-2411-043: This enables FCC to capture a richer and more nuanced understanding of the target object, improving segmentation in complex scenarios where the appearance, scale, or structure of the object in the query image differs significantly from the support images.
elements.sent-2411-045: FCC captures rich cross-layer information, enabling a network to integrate target features across multiple layers.
elements.sent-2411-046: This approach enhances the model's ability to construct a more comprehensive and robust feature representation.
elements.sent-2411-047: We design a Dual-Conditioned Fully Connected Correlation~(DCFC) to enhance the effectiveness of FCC.
elements.sent-2411-048: DCFC enables the detection of hidden target information not present in the support mask, and FCC enhances this capability even further.
elements.sent-2411-049: Extensive experiments and analyses validate the effectiveness of FCC.
elements.sent-2411-050: In PASCAL-5$^i$~\cite{pascal , COCO-20$^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft , and domain shift test, FCC performs significantly better than the existing state-of-the-art in both 1-shot and 5-shot settings.}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=22em]{sec/figures/our_fcc.png} \caption{(a) Previous methods in few-shot segmentation typically compare features at the same-level layer to generate the correlation map. (b) FCC, however, leverages all layers and their correlations to capture maximum target information. Note: For clarity, only one query feature block is visualized.} \label{fig:comparison_conv_fcc}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[height=5.9cm]{sec/figures/main7.png} \caption{(a)~The Fully Connected Correlation~(FCC) integrates cross-layer and same-layer correlations to capture comprehensive information. (b)~The support image and the masked support image are processed through the backbone to extract support and target features, which are then used to generate fully connected correlation maps, $FCC_t$ and $FCC_s$. Query features extracted from the query image contribute to calculating these correlation maps, which are then concatenated and passed through a 1x1 convolution layer to reduce the channel size. The resulting output is fed into the decoder to predict the target segmentation.} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-051: Few-Shot Segmentation: Few-shot segmentation aims to perform pixel-level classification of target objects using only a few annotated support examples.
elements.sent-2411-052: OSLSM~\cite{OSLSM is considered the first method aiming to solve the FSS problem.}
elements.sent-2411-053: OSLSM proposed a model consisting of a condition branch and a segmentation branch.
elements.sent-2411-054: Many follow-up studies have proposed various methods to address FSS~\cite{Co-FCN,AMP-2,PMM,FWB,PFENet,PANet,CANet,ASGNet,DAN,FSOT,Zhang2020SGOneSG,CWT,HSNet,ASNet,BAM,VAT2,hmmasking .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Vision Foundation Models: In recent years, vision foundation models have emerged in the field of computer vision and demonstrated impressive performance on various vision tasks.
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, SAM~\cite{SAM enables interactive image segmentation based on users' prompts.}
elements.sent-2411-057: As SAM showed robust zero-shot performance on segmentation tasks, researchers leveraged it to improve FSS performance.
elements.sent-2411-058: Matcher~\cite{liu2023matcher leveraged SAM to generate mask proposals for selecting high-quality masks, while VRP-SAM~\cite{vrpsam focused on training a network to generate prompts by learning optimal prompts for segmentation tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Painter~\cite{Painter leverages in-context learning by using redefinition of the output space of tasks as images and masked image modeling framework.}
elements.sent-2411-060: SegGPT~\cite{seggpt is another generalist model for image segmentation based on Painter.}
elements.sent-2411-061: In addition, Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining (CLIP)~\cite{clip is a multi-modal foundation model that jointly uses an image and text encoder with contrastive learning.}
elements.sent-2411-062: CLIP showed strong zero-shot performance on a variety of image classification tasks, showing potential uses in downstream vision tasks like FSS.
elements.sent-2411-063: For instance, PI-CLIP~\cite{piclip generates reliable prior information like visual-text and visual-visual prior information based on CLIP.}
elements.sent-2411-064: LLaFS~\cite{llafs leverages a text and image encoder of CLIP to employ large language models for FSS tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-065: Recently, DSCM-DFN~\cite{dscm extracts implicit knowledge from vision and vision-language foundation models to obtain fine-grained segmentation, finding the combination of DINOv2 and DFN is especially beneficial for FSS.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Previous works have leveraged pre-trained models such as SAM and CLIP, which have been trained on extensive datasets.
elements.sent-2411-067: However, this reliance on pre-trained features can hinder to adapt quickly to new data, potentially causing limited overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-068: In contrast, our proposed method, FCC with DINOv2, achieves impressive results relying solely on visual information, without the need for SAM or CLIP.
elements.sent-2411-069: Correlation in FSS: Pixel-level correlation between query features and support features can extract segmentation cues and generate prior masks.
elements.sent-2411-070: Prior Mask Guidance (PMG) is a method first proposed by \citet{tian2020prior , using pixel-level correlation to generate a prior mask.}
elements.sent-2411-071: PMG has been widely adopted in subsequent FSS methods~\cite{HSNet,VAT,hmmasking,msi,rethinking,vrpsam,piclip,dscm,ASNet , leaving correlation as a powerful and important tool in few-shot segmentation tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-072: HSNet~\cite{HSNet introduced a method called hypercorrelation, which leverages a rich set of features from the intermediate layers.}
elements.sent-2411-073: These complex correlation maps are then processed using a 4D convolutional model.
elements.sent-2411-074: Subsequently, VAT~\cite{VAT further utilizes these correlation maps through a 4D convolutional transformer.}
elements.sent-2411-075: HM~\cite{hmmasking and MSI~\cite{msi also leveraged hypercorrelation approach in FSS tasks.}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Recently, ~\citet{rethinking proposed adaptive buoys correlation to mitigate false matches by using reference features (buoys) and buoys-level correlation for FSS.}
elements.sent-2411-077: Apart from these methods, correlation maps serve as initial prior information in training networks in many FSS approaches~\cite{vrpsam,piclip .}
elements.sent-2411-078: However, we observe that the previous models only used the same-layer correlation between query and support features.
elements.sent-2411-079: This practice could be suboptimal for Vision transformer architecture as it could lose the prior knowledge existing in cross-layer correlation, especially challenging scenarios~(Fig.~\ref{fig:4cases ).}
elements.sent-2411-080: To address this issue, we propose Fully Connected Correlation (FCC) to obtain a comprehensive and informative prior mask to improve FSS performance.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[height=9.1cm]{sec/figures/challenge_cka3.png} \caption{The Centered Kernel Alignment~(CKA~\cite{pmlr-v97-kornblith19a,cka2}) similarity heatmaps between features from DINOv2-B layers illustrate the effect of target object differences. The background of the images is removed to focus solely on the target object. (a)~The CKA displays a stronger concentration along the diagonal in the entire heatmap because we compare the same features. (b)~shows the CKA between the biggest and middle-size cat. The center to the right bottom diagonal area is highlighted. (c)~shows the similarity between the biggest and the smallest cat. High-layer areas are concentrated along the diagonal, while low-to-mid layers appear to spread. (d) We simulate an occlusion scenario where the cat is obstructed by a large square. (e) We replicate the most challenging conditions, including scale variation, occlusion, shape differences, and limited information. When extracting features, low to middle layers capture different levels of detail based on variations in the object, while higher layers tend to capture more abstract, semantic representations. This results in high similarity values appearing outside of the diagonal entries. } \label{fig:cka_comp_scalediff}
elements.sent-2411-081: Fig.~\ref{fig:method illustrates the overall architecture of our proposed method (FCC).}
elements.sent-2411-082: In this architecture, DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2 serves as the backbone for feature extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-083: We extract features from two images: a masked support image and a support image.
elements.sent-2411-084: These features are used to calculate correlation maps with query features.
elements.sent-2411-085: Our method integrates both same-layer and cross-layer correlation maps between support and query features to capture a comprehensive and detailed understanding of the target object's structure, scale variations, and contextual relationships.
elements.sent-2411-086: All correlation maps are concatenated and passed through a 1x1 convolution layer to reduce channel size.
elements.sent-2411-087: We aim to build a model that can segment the target object in a query image using only a few annotated examples from the target class.
elements.sent-2411-088: Following previous approaches in FSS~\cite{HSNet, ASNet, VAT2 , we adopt an episodic training strategy for our model.}
elements.sent-2411-089: It is assumed that we are provided with two distinct image sets, $\mathcal{C _{\text{train }$ and $\mathcal{C _{\text{test }$, representing the training and testing classes, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The training dataset $\mathcal{D _{\text{train }$ is drawn from $\mathcal{C _{\text{train }$, while the testing dataset $\mathcal{D _{\text{test }$ is sampled from $\mathcal{C _\text{{test }$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Multiple episodes are constructed from both $\mathcal{D _\text{{train }$ and $\mathcal{D _\text{{test }$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-092: Each episode consists of a support set, $\mathcal{S = (I^s, M^s)$, and a query set, $\mathcal{Q = (I^q, M^q)$, having a pair of an image and mask where $I^s$ refers to an image, and $M^s$ is its corresponding mask.}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Consequently, $\mathcal{D _\text{{train = \left\{(\mathcal{S _i, \mathcal{Q _i) \right\}_{i=1 ^{N_\text{{train }$ and $\mathcal{D _\text{{test = \left\{(\mathcal{S _i, \mathcal{Q _i) \right\}_{i=1 ^{N_\text{{test }$, where $N_\text{{train }$ and $N_\text{{test }$ represent the number of episodes, used for training and testing.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: During training, we repeatedly sample episodes from $\mathcal{D _\text{{train }$ which allows the model to learn a mapping from $(I^s, M^s, I^q)$ to the query mask $M^q$.}}
elements.sent-2411-095: After training the model, we evaluate its few-shot segmentation performance on $\mathcal{D _\text{{test }$ by randomly sampling episodes without further optimization.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: We used the DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2 backbone as the image encoder, specifically employing the ViT-B/14 model, which contains 12 transformer layers.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The entire 12 layers are used to extract image features.
elements.sent-2411-098: Let \( L = \{ l_1, l_2, \ldots, l_{12 \} \) represent each of the ViT-B layers.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Intermediate features \( f_l \) were extracted from each layer \( l_i \) in \( L \), with each feature \( f_{l_i \) having the dimension $\mathbb{R ^{C \times (H/14 \times W/14)}$ for both $I^q \in \mathbb{R ^{3 \times H \times W}$ and $I^s \in \mathbb{R ^{3 \times H \times W}$ because the input image size is divided by the patch size, where the patch size is 14 for the DINO ViT-B/14 model, and C represents the hidden dimension size of the features.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Thus, we obtained a set of features \( F = \{ f_{l_1 , f_{l_2 , \ldots, f_{l_{12 \} \), capturing multi-level representations across all 12 layers.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-101: This comprehensive extraction provides a range of visual information from fine-grained details to high-level abstractions.
elements.sent-2411-102: Following the MSI process~\cite{msi , support feature~$F^s=\{f^s_{l_1 ,f^s_{l_2 , \ldots, f^s_{l_{12 $ \}, target features~$F^t=\{f^t_{l_1 ,f^t_{l_2 , \ldots, f^t_{l_{12 $ \}, and query featurs ~$F^q=\{f^q_{l_1 ,f^q_{l_2 , \ldots, f^q_{l_{12 $ \} were extracted from the 12 encoder layers respectively.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-103: The extraction of $F^t$ utilized a masked support image~($I^m \in \mathbb{R ^{ 3 \times H \times W $), isolating and preserving information pertinent solely to the target: \begin{equation}\label{eq:2} I^{m} = I^s \odot \psi (M^s),\end{equation} Where $\psi$ resizes the support mask~($M^s \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W}$) to match the dimensions of $I^s\in \mathbb{R ^{ 3 \times H \times W $, then we use the Hadamard product~($\odot$) to eliminate the background.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: This approach allows us to include all support information, enabling a comparison with $F^q$ to identify target information not available in $F^t$.
elements.sent-2411-105: Features extracted from the DINOv2-B~\cite{oquab2023dinov2 are used to calculate $FCC \in \mathbb{R ^{n^2 \times H/14 \times W/14 \times H/14 \times W/14}$ through cosine similarity between $f^t_{l_i \in F^t$ and $f^q_{l_j \in F^q$ : \begin{equation}FCC(i,j)= \cos(f^t_{l_i}, f^q_{l_j}) = \bigg(\frac{ (f^t_{l_i})^T \cdot f^q_{l_j}}{\left\| f^t_{l_i} \right\| \left\| f^q_{l_j} \right\|}\bigg),\end{equation} where \( i \) and \( j \) range from 1 to 12, representing features from layers \( l_1 \) to \( l_{12 \).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: We used ViT-B~\cite{vit as the backbone, which comprises 12 layers, but this approach can be extended to accommodate \( n \) layers for other models.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Fig.~\ref{fig:method (a) illustrates the functioning of FCC.}
elements.sent-2411-108: After this, we have 144 channels in total for across 12 layers.
elements.sent-2411-109: For \( n \) layers, this would result in $n^2$ channels.
elements.sent-2411-110: Since output of each layer in the ViT~\cite{vit has consistent channel and spatial dimensions, we can achieve FCC calculation directly.}
elements.sent-2411-111: This operation wasn’t feasible with a ResNet~\cite{Resnet backbone, which requires resizing and adjusting channel sizes.}
elements.sent-2411-112: Motivation: In few-shot segmentation, accurately identifying the target object in the query image is challenging, especially when there are variations in shape, texture, or scale between the support and query images (Fig.~\ref{fig:4cases ).}
elements.sent-2411-113: Traditional approaches rely on same-layer correlations, assuming that each layer captures a particular pattern relevant to the target.
elements.sent-2411-114: However, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:cka_comp_scalediff (a), Vision Transformers~(ViTs) display a uniform pattern in feature similarity across layers, suggesting that the relationships between layers are interconnected rather than hierarchically distinct.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Consequently, relying solely on same-layer correlation provides limited information.
elements.sent-2411-116: Higher layers retain consistent, scale-invariant features despite object variations, resulting in similar patterns across all CKA similarity heatmaps.
elements.sent-2411-117: In contrast, lower and middle layers capture varying scales and local details, leading to a broader spread in the similarity heatmap across these layers~(Fig.~\ref{fig:cka_comp_scalediff ).}
elements.sent-2411-118: This pattern indicates that cross layer correlations can provide complementary information capturing both detailed, scale dependent features and abstract, object invariant representations.
elements.sent-2411-119: By leveraging these cross-layer correlations, Fully Connected Correlation~(FCC) enriches the segmentation model's ability to recognize target objects with varying appearances, leading to improved segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-120: We designed the Dual-Condition Fully Connected Correlation~(DCFC $\in \mathbb{R ^{ (2*n^2)/4 \times H/14 \times W/14 \times H/14 \times W/14}$) to extend FCC.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Inspired by MSI~\cite{msi , we designed two pathways to extract features from the support set~(Fig.~\ref{fig:method ).}}
elements.sent-2411-122: One from the support mask~$I^s$ and the other from the masked support image~$I^m$.
elements.sent-2411-123: This approach provides a strong signal containing target-specific features~$F^t$, helping the network uncover hidden information within the features~$F^s$.
elements.sent-2411-124: Additionally, FCC is equipped for the two paths to capture fine-grained details across all layers. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \label{eq:3} FCC_{t}(i,j)= \cos(f^t_{l_i}, f^q_{l_j}), FCC_{s}(i,j)=\cos(f^s_{l_i}, f^q_{l_j}) \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $f^t_{l_i}$ and $f^s_{l_i}$ denote the target and support features, respectively, while $f^q_{l_j}$ represents the query features.
elements.sent-2411-125: We obtain $FCC_t$ and $FCC_s$ using Equation 3, concatenate them, and then pass the result through a 1x1 convolution layer to reduce the channel size.
elements.sent-2411-126: Conv1x1 convolution reduces computational overhead while allowing the network to learn meaningful representations by capturing relationships across channels.
elements.sent-2411-127: We adopted a lightweight decoder built with a depth-wise separable 4D convolution module~(DSCM)~\cite{dscm , as it effectively handles large embeddings, which is essential for our FCC.}
elements.sent-2411-128: DCFC, the reduced correlation maps by 1x1 Conv, is fed into the decoder which has been adjusted to accommodate the modified channel dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-129: Datasets: To evaluate the proposed method, FCC, we employed the PASCAL-$5^i$~\cite{pascal,pascal_2 and COCO-$20^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: We applied 4-fold testing to validate the approach, ensuring consistency with prior studies~\cite{HSNet,vrpsam,msi,hmmasking .}
elements.sent-2411-131: The classes are divided into four separate groups for training and testing purposes.
elements.sent-2411-132: For each fold, 5 classes are used for testing on PASCAL-$5^i$ and 20 classes on COCO-$20^i$.
elements.sent-2411-133: The remaining classes are used for training.
elements.sent-2411-134: This arrangement guarantees that none of the training classes are included in the test set.
elements.sent-2411-135: For the generalizability test, we train the model on COCO-$20^i$ and evaluate it on PASCAL-$5^i$.
elements.sent-2411-136: To prevent class overlap, we adjust the order of PASCAL classes in line with prior studies~\cite{HSNet,hmmasking,msi .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Implementation Details: We trained FCC using two H100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-138: For the PASCAL-$5^i$~\cite{pascal , we set the learning rate to 0.001, trained for 100 epochs, and used a batch size of 20.}
elements.sent-2411-139: For the COCO-$20^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft , we trained for 50 epochs while keeping the other settings the same as for PASCAL-$5^i$.}
elements.sent-2411-140: We used the AdamW optimizer~\cite{Adam and resized all images to 420x420 pixels.}
elements.sent-2411-141: For the loss function, we combined Dice loss with cross-entropy loss.
elements.sent-2411-142: At each epoch, we evaluated the test accuracy to identify the best-performing model.
elements.sent-2411-143: K-Shots$>$1: We obtain K corresponding mask predictions from the models with a query image and K support-set images.
elements.sent-2411-144: All predictions are summed and normalized by the highest score~\cite{HSNet,ASNet,VAT2 .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Evaluation Metrics: We report FSS performance using mean Intersection-over-Union~(mIoU) which is widely used in FSS research~\cite{FSOT,PFENet,ASGNet,HSNet,ASNet,VAT2,hmmasking .}
elements.sent-2411-146: We calculate mIoU $= \frac{1 {n}\sum_{1 ^{n IoU$ where $n$ is the number of test cases.}}}

elements.figure-2411-007: 0.8{ \begin{tabular}{l|c|cc} \multirow{2}{*}{Method} \multirow{2}{*}{Venue} \multicolumn{2}{c}{{PASCAL-5\textsuperscript{i}}} {1-shot} {5-shot} generalist model Painter*~\cite{Painter} CVPR'23 64.5 64.6 SegGPT*~\cite{seggpt} ICCV'23 83.2 89.8 specialist model HSNet \cite{HSNet} ICCV'21 66.2 70.4 HSNet* 68.7 73.8 VAT \cite{VAT} ECCV'22 67.9 72.0 VAT* 72.4 76.3 FCC this work 81.0 84.2 FCC* 86.8 88.2 \end{tabular} } \caption{Performance comparison between specialist and generalist models on PASCAL-5\textsuperscript{i}~\cite{pascal} * indicates that the categories in training cover the categories in testing. Generalist models trained on multiple datasets, not limited to PASCAL-5$^i$.} \label{table:generalist}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[height=6.4cm]{sec/figures/challenge_cases2.png} \caption{The most challenging cases, where the support mask occupies less than 5\% of the image in PASCAL-5$^i$~\cite{pascal} and COCO-20$^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft}. Ours~(FCC) with DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2} shows insightful performance compared to Baseline~\cite{dscm} in one-shot setting. } \label{fig:visual_comparison}
elements.sent-2411-147: Qualitative Results: Fig.~\ref{fig:visual_comparison presents a qualitative comparison between the Baseline~\cite{dscm and FCC.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: FCC demonstrates clear improvements, particularly in the areas where the baseline struggles to segment the target object.
elements.sent-2411-149: We observed that FCC can achieve accurate segment consistently even in the cases where the support mask size is significantly larger or smaller than the query mask.
elements.sent-2411-150: COCO-$20^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft : Table~\ref{table:performance_COCO illustrates the performance of FCC on the COCO-$20^i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: FCC demonstrates significant improvements compared to previous models, achieving 65.7 mIoU in the 1-shot setting and 69.9 mIoU in the 5-shot setting.
elements.sent-2411-152: PASCAL-$5^i$~\cite{pascal : Table~\ref{table:performance_pascal presents the performance of FCC and SOTA on the PASCAL-5$^i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: FCC demonstrates a substantial improvement over all other models, both vision-only and vision-language models.
elements.sent-2411-154: FCC achieved over 80.0 mIoU in both 1-shot and 5-shot settings.
elements.sent-2411-155: In particular, the 5-shot performance of FCC shows significant improvements over other models.
elements.sent-2411-156: FCC outperforms even class-aware methods e.g., DSCM-DFN~\cite{dscm and PI-CLIP~\cite{piclip .}}
elements.sent-2411-157: Generalization Test: We evaluated our model's generalization by training it on the COCO-$20^i$ and testing it on the PASCAL-$5^i$~(Table~\ref{table:performance_pascal_shift ).}
elements.sent-2411-158: The results show that the high performance achieved on COCO-$20^i$ effectively transfers to PASCAL-$5^i$.
elements.sent-2411-159: Notably, the classes used for training on COCO-$20^i$ do not overlap with the classes present in the PASCAL-$5^i$ test set for this study.
elements.sent-2411-160: Comparison with Generalist Methods: In Table~\ref{table:generalist , we present the performance comparison between FCC, generalist models, and other specialized models on the PASCAL-$5^i$.}
elements.sent-2411-161: In this study, FCC was retrained on the training data, including test classes.
elements.sent-2411-162: The results showed that FCC outperforms all state-of-the-art specialized methods and generalist models in 1-shot setting.
elements.sent-2411-163: In 5-shot setting, FCC is the second-best model followed by SegGPT (88.2 mIoU vs 89.8 mIoU).
elements.sent-2411-164: However, it is important to know that SegGPT was trained on multiple datasets, not limited to PASCAL-$5^i$.
elements.sent-2411-165: Why FCC is useful: FCC, encompassing both cross-layer and same-layer pixel-level correlations, is particularly beneficial when support and query images have different shapes.
elements.sent-2411-166: FCC enables the model to exploit multi-scale feature representations by comparing various layers, capturing finer and broader similarities.
elements.sent-2411-167: To evaluate the effectiveness of our method, we conducted a comprehensive analysis.
elements.sent-2411-168: Table~\ref{abstudy_fcc reveals that incorporating FCC consistently enhances segmentation quality.}
elements.sent-2411-169: When both FCC and DCFC were combined, the mIoU reached a peak of 81.0, indicating that the baseline model~\cite{dscm does not fully leverage the information FCC provides.}
elements.figure-2411-009: 0.8{ \begin{tabular}{c|ccc|c} \makecell{Method \& Backbone} {Baseline~\cite{dscm}} {FCC} {DCFC} {mIoU} \multirow{4}{*}{\makecell{FCC (DINOv2)}} \checkmark $\cdot$ $\cdot$ 76.8 \checkmark \checkmark $\cdot$ 79.2 \checkmark $\cdot$ \checkmark 80.2 \checkmark \checkmark \checkmark 81.0 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study about FCC and DCFC on PASCAL-5$^i$~\cite{pascal}} \label{abstudy_fcc}
elements.sent-2411-170: The results suggest that higher layers capture stable scale invariant information while lower layers focus on finer scale sensitive details.
elements.sent-2411-171: This pattern occurs because low to middle layers are tuned to capture localized textures, edges, and patterns that change with object differences, resulting in more dispersed similarity across these layers.
elements.sent-2411-172: In contrast, higher layers capture more abstract and semantic meaning, even under scale variation.
elements.sent-2411-173: These insights confirm FCC’s essential role in leveraging both detailed and abstract information through cross-layer correlations, effectively enhancing segmentation performance when an object's shape varies between support and query images.
elements.sent-2411-174: ResNet50 exhibits strong similarity within its lower layers, but this similarity gradually decreases across layers, highlighting its hierarchical structure, which limits cross-layer comparison.
elements.sent-2411-175: In contrast, DINOv2 maintains consistent similarity across layers, indicating a more uniform representation that balances local and global information.
elements.sent-2411-176: This uniformity allows FCC with DINOv2 to capture richer, multi-scale features, enhancing segmentation performance across varied object scales.
elements.sent-2411-177: This visualization demonstrates the usefulness of FCC, leveraging both same-level with cross-level correlations to obtain comprehensive target information.
elements.sent-2411-178: Determining the Optimal Number of Layers: To determine the optimal number of layers for calculating correlations, we performed extensive experiments using various layer combinations~(Table~\ref{table:cross_level_ab ).}
elements.sent-2411-179: The numbers indicate the range of layers used for cross-layer correlation calculation.
elements.sent-2411-180: For instance, Cross3 includes the comparison between the same layer, one above, and one below the current level of layer.
elements.sent-2411-181: Ultimately, we found that fully utilizing cross-correlation provides the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-182: The Most Challenging Cases: Table~\ref{table:Ablation_study_small demonstrates that FCC significantly outperforms the Baseline~\cite{dscm , especially in the most challenging scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Notably, it improves performance on small size support masks by 7.3 mIoU on PASCAL-$5^i$ and 9.9 on COCO-$20^i$, which is considerably higher than the overall improvements of 3.6 mIoU on PASCAL-$5^i$ and 7.2 mIoU on COCO-$20^i$.
elements.sent-2411-184: This indicates that FCC enhances segmentation quality overall, with substantial gains in difficult cases.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{minipage}{.47\textwidth} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}cc|cccc|c} \multirow{2}{*}{Backbone} \multirow{2}{*}{Methods} \multicolumn{5}{c}{1-shot} $20^0$ $20^1$ $20^2$ $20^3$ mIoU \multirow{5}{*}{DINOv2~\cite{Resnet}} Baseline~\cite{dscm} 76.5 81.3 72.1 77.4 76.8 FCC~(Cross3) 79.2 82.8 74.2 77.7 78.5 FCC~(Dilated Cross3) 79.4 82.7 73.1 {78.4} {78.4} FCC~(Cross5) 79.6 82.7 74.3 77.7 78.6 FCC~(Fully Cross) 80.4 83.0 74.7 78.5 79.2 \end{tabular} } \end{minipage} \caption{ Training FCC on PASCAL-5$i$~\cite{pascal}. For this experiment, we removed DCFC. The number specifies the number of layers included in the cross-layer correlation calculation.} \label{table:cross_level_ab}
elements.figure-2411-011: \begin{minipage}{.41\textwidth} \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{@{}cc|cccc|c} \multirow{2}{*}{Backbone} \multirow{2}{*}{Methods} \multicolumn{5}{c}{1-shot} $20^0$ $20^1$ $20^2$ $20^3$ mIoU \multirow{2}{*}{\makecell{DINOv2 (PASCAL-5$^i$)}} Baseline~\cite{dscm} 73.8 63.9 61.5 61.0 65.1 FCC~(Ours) 78.6 69.2 68.7 72.9 72.4 \multirow{2}{*}{\makecell{DINOv2 (COCO-20$^i$)}} Baseline~\cite{dscm} 51.6 50.0 55.2 49.1 51.5 FCC~(Ours) 60.1 61.2 63.5 60.6 61.4 \end{tabular} } \end{minipage} \caption{ Performance comparison on PASCAL-5$^i$~\cite{pascal} and COCO-20$^i$~\cite{lin2015microsoft} for the most challenging cases where support masks occupy below 5\% of the support images. Best results in bold. } \label{table:Ablation_study_small}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=22em]{sec/figures/cka_comp2.png} \caption{CKA Similarity Heatmap comparison between ResNet50~\cite{Resnet} and DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2}. Features extracted from the same object. (a)~shows high similarity among the early layers of the network. As the layers go deeper, the similarity gradually decreases. (b)~displays a more uniform similarity distribution across layers. This suggests that DINOv2~\cite{oquab2023dinov2} maintains a consistent representation across its layers. } \label{fig:resnet}
elements.figure-2411-013: \includegraphics[height=3.5cm]{sec/figures/channel_weight5.png} \caption{Heatmap visualization of correlation prioritization through 1x1 convolution weights in FCC. The heatmap shows how specific correlations are emphasized in different channels. This selective weighting directs FCC’s focus to the most relevant features, enhancing segmentation accuracy in complex scenarios.} \label{fig:channel_weight}
elements.sent-2411-185: We introduced FCC, a fully connected correlation approach for few-shot segmentation, which delivered an impressive performance, achieving high-level segmentation accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-186: We validated the effectiveness of FCC on the PASCAL-$5^i$ and COCO-$20^i$ datasets, even under domain shift conditions.
elements.sent-2411-187: FCC shows potential for application in other domain-specific tasks that require similar capabilities.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: After receiving paper reviews, authors may optionally submit a rebuttal to address the reviewers' comments, which will be limited to a {\bf one page PDF file.}
elements.sent-2411-189: Please follow the steps and style guidelines outlined below for submitting your author response.
elements.sent-2411-190: The author rebuttal is optional and, following similar guidelines to previous conferences, is meant to provide you with an opportunity to rebut factual errors or to supply additional information requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-191: It is NOT intended to add new contributions (theorems, algorithms, experiments) that were absent in the original submission and NOT specifically requested by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-192: You may optionally add a figure, graph, or proof to your rebuttal to better illustrate your answer to the reviewers' comments.
elements.sent-2411-193: Per a passed 2018 PAMI-TC motion, reviewers should refrain from requesting significant additional experiments for the rebuttal or penalize for lack of additional experiments.
elements.sent-2411-194: Authors should refrain from including new experimental results in the rebuttal, especially when not specifically requested to do so by the reviewers.
elements.sent-2411-195: Authors may include figures with illustrations or comparison tables of results reported in the submission/supplemental material or in other papers.
elements.sent-2411-196: Just like the original submission, the rebuttal must maintain anonymity and cannot include external links that reveal the author identity or circumvent the length restriction.
elements.sent-2411-197: The rebuttal must comply with this template (the use of sections is not required, though it is recommended to structure the rebuttal for ease of reading).
elements.sent-2411-198: Author responses must be no longer than 1 page in length including any references and figures.
elements.sent-2411-199: Overlength responses will simply not be reviewed.
elements.sent-2411-200: This includes responses where the margins and formatting are deemed to have been significantly altered from those laid down by this style guide.
elements.sent-2411-201: Note that this \LaTeX\ guide already sets figure captions and references in a smaller font.
elements.sent-2411-202: All text must be in a two-column format.
elements.sent-2411-203: The total allowable size of the text area is $6\frac78$ inches (17.46 cm) wide by $8\frac78$ inches (22.54 cm) high.
elements.sent-2411-204: Columns are to be $3\frac14$ inches (8.25 cm) wide, with a $\frac{5 {16}$ inch (0.8 cm) space between them.}
elements.sent-2411-205: The top margin should begin 1 inch (2.54 cm) from the top edge of the page.
elements.sent-2411-206: The bottom margin should be $1\frac{1 {8}$ inches (2.86 cm) from the bottom edge of the page for $8.5 \times 11$-inch paper; for A4 paper, approximately $1\frac{5 {8}$ inches (4.13 cm) from the bottom edge of the page.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: It is important for readers to be able to refer to any particular equation.
elements.sent-2411-209: Wherever Times is specified, Times Roman may also be used.
elements.sent-2411-210: Main text should be in 10-point Times, single-spaced.
elements.sent-2411-211: Section headings should be in 10 or 12 point Times.
elements.sent-2411-212: All paragraphs should be indented 1 pica (approx.~$\frac{1 {6}$ inch or 0.422 cm).}
elements.sent-2411-213: Figure and table captions should be 9-point Roman type as in \cref{fig:onecol .}
elements.sent-2411-214: List and number all bibliographical references in 9-point Times, single-spaced, at the end of your response.
elements.sent-2411-215: When referenced in the text, enclose the citation number in square brackets, for example~\cite{Alpher05 .}
elements.sent-2411-216: Where appropriate, include the name(s) of editors of referenced books.
elements.figure-2411-014: \fbox{\rule{0pt}{0.5in} \rule{0.9\linewidth}{0pt}} \caption{Example of caption. It is set in Roman so that mathematics (always set in Roman: $B \sin A = A \sin B$) may be included without an ugly clash.} \label{fig:onecol}
elements.sent-2411-217: To avoid ambiguities, it is best if the numbering for equations, figures, tables, and references in the author response does not overlap with that in the main paper (the reviewer may wonder if you talk about \cref{fig:onecol in the author response or in the paper).}
elements.sent-2411-220: Please ensure that any point you wish to make is resolvable in a printed copy of the response.
elements.sent-2411-221: Resize fonts in figures to match the font in the body text, and choose line widths which render effectively in print.
elements.sent-2411-222: Readers (and reviewers), even of an electronic copy, may choose to print your response in order to read it.
elements.sent-2411-223: You cannot insist that they do otherwise, and therefore must not assume that they can zoom in to see tiny details on a graphic.
elements.sent-2411-224: When placing figures in \LaTeX, it is almost always best to use \verb+\includegraphics+, and to specify the figure width as a multiple of the line width as in the example below { .}
